result:
Filled 35 decisions for the kit-contributed rig templates writ.

**Summary of the shape of the answers:**

- **Naming (D1, D10, D17)** — qualified for plugin-internal identifiers (rig template names, following Loom precedent), unqualified for user-facing identifiers (writ type names, and mapping keys which must match). Principle #22's reader-naming test splits these.

- **Structure (D2, D12, D29)** — class registry for Spider (matches in-file BlockTypeRegistry precedent #13); inline Set for Clerk (single consumer, principle #18); unified SpiderKit interface over split surfaces.

- **Config override (D5, D13)** — lookup-time precedence over silent-registration-skip, to preserve structural state (#20) and avoid silent drift (#2).

- **Validation asymmetry (D8, D23, D24, D34)** — consistent control-axis split: operator-authored config throws; plugin-authored kit contributions warn-and-skip. Tracks who can fix the input.

- **Apparatus pre-scan (D4, D14, D31)** — all answered (b): stick with current Spider pattern, don't build the apparatus-supportKit slot without a named consumer (#18).

- **D27 high-confidence flip on semantics** — chose (b) to redefine `rigTemplates` keys as pure template names and split bindings into `rigTemplateMappings`. Principle #30's dimensional-split argument. This cascaded into D21(b) and D35(b).

- **One medium-confidence cluster worth flagging**: D6 (Spider owns its own designId→pluginId map vs. extending FabricatorApi) — the choice turns on whether teaching the Fabricator about source-plugins earns its keep, which I couldn't verify without the codebase.

No `custom` answers, no `no_principle_fired` abstentions — the principle set covered this surface cleanly.
